summary: Gruber reviews Halide, a new iPhone camera app that distinguishes itself through exceptional design rather than unique features. He acknowledges the difficulty of competing in the crowded camera app market, especially against Apple's built-in Camera app. While Halide offers advanced photography features like manual focus, RAW support, and a live histogram, none are unique to it. What sets Halide apart is its obsessive attention to how controls feel and are arranged, culminating in a commissioned custom typeface inspired by Leica and Zeiss lens inscriptions. Gruber argues this level of craft is what separates great software from merely functional software.
key_points: Creating a new camera app in 2017 requires competing against hundreds of alternatives plus Apple's built-in Camera app with its lock screen access advantage
Halide succeeds by focusing exclusively on still photography, freeing screen space for advanced controls that the point-and-shoot Camera app can't prioritize
The app's features (histogram, manual focus, RAW, focus peaking) aren't unique — what differentiates it is the design of how those features are accessed and arranged
Commissioning a custom typeface inspired by Leica and Zeiss lens inscriptions signals the level of craft invested in the product
There's a way to do modern UI design while keeping whimsy, texture, and depth — Halide demonstrates this balance
money_quotes: It takes some real grit to create a new iPhone camera app in 2017.
What sets Halide apart is design.
Halide brings that sort of obsessive attention to the placement and feel of its controls. This sort of maniacal attention to the smallest of details deserves to be celebrated.
There's a way to do modern UI design while keeping some whimsy, texture, and depth — and Halide is a wonderful example of how to do it right.
The fact that Sandofsky and de With went so far as to commission a custom typeface is probably all you need to hear about Halide.

Some of you will hear that and think 'That's insane, why would anyone waste so much time and effort on a custom typeface just for a few UI controls?' The rest of you are like me, and will think, 'That's insane, I need to check this out immediately.'
key_insight: In a market saturated with functionally similar products, obsessive attention to design details — how features feel, not just what they do — is what separates great software from the rest.
